channel.title: The Hungarian Daily
channel.description: Your essential daily English-language briefing on Hungarian politics, business, and culture. Fully automated with AI.
channel.itunes:author: Eric Chi

item.title: The Hungarian Daily — 2026-04-14
item.description: Good morning, afternoon, or evening, depending on where youre tuning in from! And a very warm welcome to The Hungarian Daily, your essential podcast for foreign professionals, expats, digital nomads, and international business executives navigating life and work here in Budapest, Hungary.It is Monday, April 13, 2026, and what a seismic shift we have to discuss today. If youre just waking up, or perhaps still reeling from the weekends events, buckle up. Hungary has just witnessed a political earthquake of historic proportions, and the tremors are already being felt across the economy and beyond.Our top story, dominating every headline and conversation, is the **landslide victory of Péter Magyar** (Pay-ter Mah-djar) and his Tisza (Tih-sah) Party in yesterday’s parliamentary elections. This outcome has swept away the Orbán (Or-bahn) era, bringing to an end Viktor Orbáns remarkable 16-year tenure as Prime Minister. Its a moment that many thought might never come, and the capital, Budapest (Boo-da-pesht), erupted into spontaneous celebrations last night, with Reuters reporting that the city truly turned into a party zone as the news broke.

item.title: The Hungarian Daily — 2026-04-15
item.description: Jó reggelt, Boo-da-pesht! And a very warm welcome to The Hungarian Daily, your essential podcast for foreign professionals, expats, digital nomads, and international business executives navigating life and work in Hungary. I’m your host, and it is Wednesday, April 15, 2026. Lets dive into the headlines that are shaping our world here in Hungary today.We are kicking off with what can only be described as a seismic shift in Hungarian politics. After 16 years at the helm, Prime Minister Viktor Orbán has conceded defeat following what international media outlets like PBS, NPR, and the BBC are calling a painful election result and a landslide for the opposition. The Tisza (Tee-sa) Party, led by the charismatic Péter Magyar (Pay-ter Mah-djar), has reportedly secured a dominant mandate, with some reports even suggesting a two-thirds majority in Parliament. This is an absolutely monumental development, signaling the end of an era and the beginning of a new chapter for Hungary.Péter Magyar (Pay-ter Mah-djar) has wasted no time in making his intentions clear. In a series of high-profile interviews on state media, including Kossuth (Koh-shoot) Rádió and M1, his first appearances there in over a year, he delivered a stark message. He stated that after 16 years of the Orbán (Or-bahn) government, a staggering 400,000 Hungarian children are living in deep poverty. This is a powerful statement, setting a clear agenda for social change. Furthermore, in a move that will resonate deeply with international observers and press freedom advocates, Magyar (Mah-djar) pledged that one of his first steps would be to suspend the news service of this propaganda media, referring to the state-controlled outlets. This promise of media reform, after years of criticism regarding press freedom in Hungary, marks a significant departure from the previous administrations approach. He also reassured the public that everything that is a good measure regarding family support would be maintained, indicating a selective approach to policy changes rather than a wholesale dismantling of existing programs.
